original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token as Underlying Creates Accounting Mismatch and Last-Withdrawer Loss
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: requestDeposit records the full `assets` parameter into queuedDeposit and depositEpochState.assetsDeposited, but SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom transfers fewer tokens to the vault if the underlying is a fee-on-transfer token. The vault's actual balance is less than the recorded deposits, creating a growing shortfall. Early withdrawers receive full entitlements, while later withdrawers face insufficient balance and reverted transfers.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The contract performs no received-amount validation after the transfer. If deployed with a fee-on-transfer underlying token, the accounting mismatch grows with every deposit, eventually causing insolvency. The documentation states 'Valid EIP-20 tokens' which technically includes fee-on-transfer tokens.
deployment_assumptions: underlyingToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC-20 token
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: HypoVault.sol constructor accepts any address as underlyingToken. No balance-before/after check in requestDeposit. Project docs say 'Valid EIP-20 tokens' but fee-on-transfer is EIP-20 compliant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuedDeposit += assets; safeTransferFrom(assets) without checking actual received amount; no balance delta validation

preconditions: underlyingToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC-20 token
Multiple users deposit over time, accumulating shortfall
Users execute withdrawals, draining actual balance faster than recorded

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: queuedDeposit[user][epoch] += assets records nominal amount; vault receives assets - fee
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Growing accounting shortfall between recorded deposits and actual balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early withdrawers extract full value; last withdrawers face insolvency

economic_questions: What fee-on-transfer tokens are likely deployment candidates?
How quickly does the shortfall grow relative to deposit volume?
Can an attacker deliberately exploit the shortfall by timing withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test requestDeposit with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Calculate shortfall accumulation rate for typical fee percentages
Verify executeWithdrawal behavior when vault balance < reservedWithdrawalAssets

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to total accumulated fee shortfall extractable by early withdrawers
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Equal to the transfer fee percentage accumulated across all deposits

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Early withdrawal before shortfall causes reverts

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Last withdrawers unable to execute withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative transfer fees across all deposits

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Candidate underlying tokens and their fee structures
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model shortfall growth for common fee-on-transfer rates

validation_result.mechanism: Missing received-amount validation for fee-on-transfer tokens
validation_result.impact_summary: If deployed with a fee-on-transfer underlying token, the vault's recorded deposit amounts exceed its actual token balance, creating a growing shortfall that causes later withdrawers to face reverted transfers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The requestDeposit function records the full `assets` parameter into both `queuedDeposit[msg.sender][currentEpoch]` and `depositEpochState[currentEpoch].assetsDeposited` before calling `SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom`. No balance-before/after check is performed. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the vault receives `assets - transferFee` but records `assets`, creating an accounting mismatch. This shortfall accumulates with every deposit. When withdrawals are fulfilled and users call executeWithdrawal, the vault attempts to transfer out the full calculated amount, but the actual token balance is less than the sum of all recorded deposits minus executed withdrawals. Eventually, the vault becomes insolvent for late withdrawers. Additionally, cancelDeposit attempts to return the full nominal queuedDepositAmount, which would also fail if the vault's actual balance is insufficient. The constructor accepts any address as underlyingToken with no validation of token behavior, making this a code defect in the absence of received-amount validation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines in requestDeposit clearly show the pattern: state is updated with the nominal `assets` value, then safeTransferFrom is called without checking how much was actually received. No deployment scripts specify which tokens are acceptable, and the constructor has no token behavior validation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function records `assets` (the nominal transfer amount) in state before calling safeTransferFrom. If the underlying token charges a fee on transfer, the vault receives fewer tokens than recorded. No balance-before/after check validates the actual amount received.

cancelDeposit attempts to return the full nominal queuedDepositAmount, but if the token charged a fee on the original deposit transfer, the vault holds fewer tokens than recorded. A single deposit+cancel cycle would fail if the vault has no surplus tokens.

executeWithdrawal transfers assetsToWithdraw calculated from the inflated recorded deposits. When the actual vault balance is less than the sum of all recorded but unexecuted withdrawal entitlements, late withdrawers face reverted transfers.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls requestDeposit(100) with a fee-on-transfer token (2% fee)
4. SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom transfers 100 from user, vault receives 98 (2 fee burned/collected)
5. Vault state says 100 deposited, vault actually holds 98
6. Multiple users deposit, shortfall grows (e.g., 10 users deposit 100 each: recorded 1000, actual 980)
7. Manager calls fulfillDeposits - NAV includes actual balance (980) but state says 1000 deposited
8. Users call executeWithdrawal - assetsToWithdraw calculations based on inflated recorded amounts
9. Last withdrawers face safeTransfer reverts when vault balance is insufficient
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: requestDeposit records `assets` in queuedDeposit (line 231) and depositEpochState.assetsDeposited (line 233), then calls safeTransferFrom (line 235). No balance delta check exists between lines 233 and 235. The shortfall propagates through fulfillDeposits (line 447: epochState.assetsDeposited used in totalAssets calculation) and executeWithdrawal (line 370: safeTransfer of calculated assetsToWithdraw).

deployment_assumptions_checked: underlyingToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC-20 token
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. foundry.toml and remappings.txt contain only build configuration, no token address specifications. Constructor accepts any address as _underlyingToken with no validation of token behavior.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: (1) Deploy or use vault with a fee-on-transfer underlying token, (2) Multiple users deposit over time, each deposit recording nominal amount but vault receiving less, (3) Shortfall accumulates proportionally to total deposit volume × fee rate, (4) When users execute withdrawals, early withdrawers succeed but later withdrawers face insufficient balance. Even without malicious intent, the accounting mismatch creates insolvency. A deliberate attacker could deposit large amounts early, get shares based on inflated recorded deposits, then withdraw early to extract more than their fair share, leaving later users unable to withdraw.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the vault is deployed with a fee-on-transfer token: (1) Every deposit creates a shortfall of (assets × fee_rate), (2) The shortfall is irreversible and accumulates, (3) cancelDeposit would also fail for exact-amount returns, (4) Last withdrawers lose up to 100% of their withdrawal entitlement, (5) Total value at risk equals the cumulative transfer fees across all deposits. For a 1% fee-on-transfer token with $10M total deposits, the shortfall would be $100K.
recommended_mitigations: Add balance-before/after check in requestDeposit: uint256 balanceBefore = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)); safeTransferFrom(...); uint256 received = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)) - balanceBefore; record 'received' instead of 'assets'
Alternatively, explicitly revert if the received amount differs from the requested amount, enforcing standard-only token compatibility
Document and validate at construction time that the underlying token does not charge transfer fees
Apply the same received-amount validation pattern in cancelDeposit and any other transfer-out paths
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC-20 with a 2% fee on transfer. 2. Deploy HypoVault with this token as underlyingToken. 3. Have User A deposit 1000 tokens. Verify vault balance is 980 but queuedDeposit records 1000. 4. Have User B deposit 1000 tokens. Vault balance is 1960, recorded deposits are 2000. 5. Manager fulfills deposits and then withdrawals. 6. User A executes withdrawal successfully. 7. User B's executeWithdrawal reverts due to insufficient balance.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol intends to ever deploy with fee-on-transfer tokens (no documentation available beyond 'Valid EIP-20 tokens')
The exact SafeTransferLib implementation - whether it has any built-in fee-on-transfer protection (unlikely based on standard implementations)
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with mock fee-on-transfer token to verify shortfall accumulation rate
Test cancelDeposit path with fee-on-transfer token to confirm revert on exact refund
Test edge case where NAV computation sees actual (lower) balance but state records higher deposits
